Description:
*Oops i did it again!*

Solution:
We given n, e , and c for RSA decryption mission.
When you normally try to crack it using RsaCtfTool:

```bash
┌──(venv)─(kali㉿kali)-[~/Downloads/RsaCtfTool]
└─$ RsaCtfTool -n  -e 65537 --decrypt  
private argument is not set, the private key will not be displayed, even if recovered.
['/tmp/tmp69vq8rly']

[*] Testing key /tmp/tmp69vq8rly.
attack initialized...
attack initialized...
[!] Your provided modulus is prime:
                   
There is no need to run an integer factorization... 
```

So basically you can't factor a prime into p and q to get phi(n) which equals (p-1)(q-1), so here comes **Fermat’s little theorem**:
`phi(n)=n-1 : n is prime`

It actually a big security flaw to pick n as prime as it will make easy to get phi and though... easily crack the inverse modulo of e. (`d = e^{-1} mod (n−1)`)

Websites used:
https://www.alpertron.com.ar/ECM.HTM (factor n to p and q or check if it's prime and many others)
https://planetcalc.com/3298/ (get the modular inverse using Extended Euclidean algorithm)

Open the second link and type:

![](images/Pasted%20image%2020251102230952.png)

First integer: e
Second integer: n
Coefficient for smaller integer: d
Now go calculate `m = c^d mod n`

You could automate the whole process with python script:

```python
n = 
e = 65537
c = 
phi = n - 1
d = pow(e, -1, phi)
m = pow(c, d, n)
hexm = format(m, 'x')
print(bytes.fromhex(hexm).decode())
```

Flag:
**v1t{f3rm4t_l1ttl3_duck}**
